title: Chapter 1: The Drop-Off
content: The car smelled like sandalwood and betrayal.

Chloe Hart stared at the wrought-iron gates of Serenity Peak Wellness Center and felt something twist in her chest. Not fear. Recognition. She'd seen places like this before — on Reddit threads about cults, in documentaries about private prisons with spa menus. The same architecture of control dressed in farmhouse chic.
"Beautiful, isn't it?" Jake said from the driver's seat, his hands at ten and two like he was driving her to a picnic.
"It looks like an AirBnB that charges you your civil rights."
He laughed. That easy, condescending laugh she'd heard a thousand times. The laugh that said *you're being dramatic, Chloe*. The laugh that had signed the commitment papers.
Her mother, in the passenger seat, adjusted her sunglasses. "We're doing this because we love you, sweetheart."
The word "sweetheart" had never sounded more like a threat.
Chloe watched the gates swing open. They moved too smoothly. Hydraulic. Expensive. The kind of gate that kept people in, not out.
"You haven't said a word since Newark," Jake said.
"I was counting how many times you'd check your phone. Twenty-three. You're very busy destroying someone's life."
He didn't respond to that. He never did when she landed one.

The intake lounge was a study in calculated comfort. Exposed brick. A gas fireplace that wasn't burning. Leather chairs arranged in a circle, like a support group for people who hadn't realized they needed support yet.
A woman in her late fifties stood by the reception desk, clipboard in hand. She wore a cream-colored cardigan and smiled like someone who'd practiced the expression in a mirror.
"Chloe. I'm Nurse Beatrice Cross. Welcome."
Her voice was soft. Warm. The voice of someone who'd spent decades learning exactly how warm to sound before warmth became a weapon.
"Thanks. Do I get a welcome basket? I was promised brochures."
Nurse Cross's smile didn't flicker. "Your parents and Jake have filled us in on your situation. We're here to help you find your footing."
"My 'situation.' You mean the part where I refused to work eighty hours a week for my father's company, or the part where I broke up with a man who thought 'no' was a negotiation starting point?"
"All of it." Nurse Cross's voice stayed level. "You're carrying a lot of pain. This is a safe space to unpack it."
Chloe smiled. "Great. I brought my emotional baggage. It's got wheels. Very efficient."
They took her phone. Of course they took her phone. And her laptop. And her books — "We have our own library, dear."
They gave her a welcome packet. It was printed on heavy paper, the kind that cost money. The cover read: *Your Journey to Wholeness Begins Here.*
Inside was a tier system. Red. Yellow. Green. Gold.
Red meant maximum supervision. Yellow meant limited privileges. Green meant you were "progressing." Gold meant you could leave.
Chloe read it twice. Then she laughed.
"What's funny?" asked the orderly who was escorting her to Ward B. He was young, early twenties, with the hollow-eyed look of someone who'd been told this was a good job and was starting to doubt it.
"It's a credit score. For your soul."
He didn't laugh. But his mouth twitched.
Ward B was beige. Beige walls. Beige floors. Beige curtains that didn't quite cover the reinforced windows. It smelled like bleach and lavender, a combination that made Chloe's nose itch.
Six beds. Four occupied.

In the far corner, a girl of about seventeen sat cross-legged on her bed, typing furiously on a tablet that was definitely not facility-issued. Her hair was a curtain of black silk, and she didn't look up when Chloe entered.
In the bed nearest the door, another girl — a teenager in designer pajamas — was curled into a ball, her knees to her chest. She was crying silently.
Chloe took the empty bed by the window. She sat down. She pressed her palm against the glass.
It wasn't just cold. It was double-paned. Reinforced.
*You're not leaving through this window,* she thought. *So you'll have to leave another way.*
Dinner was served at six. A quinoa bowl with roasted vegetables. It was objectively good food. Chloe ate it slowly, watching.
The crying girl from the bed was at a table by herself, pushing food around her plate without eating.
Chloe walked over. Sat down.
"The quinoa's okay. The kale is lying to you."

The girl looked up. She had the kind of face that was used to apologizing for existing — wide eyes, hunched shoulders, the perpetual flinch of someone who'd been trained to take up less space. "I'm not — I can't — they said I have to finish, but I haven't eaten solids in — "
"Days?"
"Eleven days. They said I have an eating disorder."
"Do you?"

Tiffany's eyes flickered. "I don't know anymore. I think I just wanted to control one thing. Just one." She pressed her palm against the table. A small, determined gesture. "And now they took that too."
"What's your name?"
"Tiffany."
"Tiffany. I'm Chloe. And I'm going to tell you a secret."
Tiffany's eyes widened.
"Nobody here can make you do anything. They can make you feel like crap. They can take your phone. But they can't make you eat kale. That's a choice. And it's yours."
Tiffany stared at her for a long moment. Then, very slowly, she pushed the bowl away.
It was the smallest act of defiance Chloe had ever seen. But it was something.
Later that night, the beige lights dimmed. Chloe lay in bed, staring at the ceiling, listening to the sounds of the ward. Someone crying. Someone talking in their sleep. The quiet hum of cameras.
A voice came out of the darkness. The typing girl's voice.
"You're not crazy."
Chloe turned her head. The girl was sitting up, her tablet screen casting a pale glow on her face.
"Neither are you."
The girl's mouth curved. "No. I'm just inconvenient."
"Best diagnosis I've heard all day."
"Mia."
"Chloe."
They looked at each other across the dark ward. Two strangers who'd just recognized each other as allies.
"The cameras have a blind spot," Mia said. "The bathroom. Last stall. I've been mapping the network."
"You hacked the facility from your bed?"
"I got bored. The firewall's a joke. It's called 'PeaceNet' and it runs on a default admin password."
Chloe smiled into the darkness. It was the first real smile she'd felt in months.
"Mia. I think this is the beginning of a beautiful friendship."
Day one at Serenity Peak Wellness Center.
Chloe had lost her phone, her freedom, and her ability to order takeout. But she'd gained something too.
A mission.
She was going to burn this place to the ground.
But first, she needed to sleep. Tomorrow, the real work began.

She closed her eyes and listened to the beige hum of the ventilation system. Somewhere, a camera was watching her. Somewhere, Nurse Cross was probably reviewing her intake notes, looking for weaknesses.
Let her look. Chloe had spent twenty-two years building armor. A week in a luxury prison wasn't going to crack it.
The last thing she heard before sleep was Mia's fingers on the tablet keyboard — soft, rapid, relentless. Like a countdown.
**Next: Chapter 2 — The Welcome Packet**
